title: Test Plans
description: Learn how test plans serve as blueprints for testing, work with trackers and parent-child relationships, and structure test cases within the project.

topics.title: Test Plans and Strategy

content.text: Test leads write test plans to document the strategy used to verify the product meets specified requirements. A test plan is a blueprint for testing — it outlines which requirements to test, when to test them, and who is responsible for each aspect of the testing effort.

A test plan acts as a framework for outlining the methodology for testing a product and ensuring validation of all its specified requirements. Products are comprised of smaller functional units — hardware components, software modules, and their integrations. Testing these components as they are built is vital for ensuring the full product works as expected.

cards.back: Defines the overall approach to testing — what types of tests to perform, which tools to use, and how to manage risk. The strategy is company-dependent and may follow Agile, V-model, or ASPICE.
Identifies which requirements are in scope for testing and which are out of scope. Ensures all critical requirements have corresponding test coverage.
Defines when testing occurs — during development sprints, at integration milestones, or before release. Aligns testing activities with the overall project timeline.
Assigns testing work to specific roles. The test lead manages the plan, test engineers design cases, and testers execute them.

content.text: Test plans are typically written and managed by the test lead. This course does not cover how to create test plans from scratch, but understanding their purpose is essential because test engineers reference them when writing test cases.

In the test management workflow, the test plan is the first deliverable. The test lead creates the plan, uploads it to the Codebeamer project, and then assigns workflow tasks to team members. Test engineers use the plan as their guide for writing test cases.

content.prompt: Arrange the test plan activities in the correct order:
items: Upload the test plan to the Codebeamer project
Assign workflow tasks to team members
Test engineers reference the plan to write test cases

keyTakeaways: A test plan is a blueprint that outlines testing strategies, scope, schedule, and responsibilities
Test leads write and manage test plans; test engineers use them to guide test case creation
Test plans are company-dependent and may follow Agile, V-model, or ASPICE methodologies
The test plan is the first deliverable in the test management workflow

topics.title: Trackers and Parent-Child Relationships

content.text: In Codebeamer, project data is organized at the highest level into trackers. Trackers provide logical containers for your data where you can define data structure with fields, configure permissions, state transitions, actions, and notifications.

rows: Stores customer, system, hardware, and software requirements
Contains test procedures with steps that verify requirements
Records the execution of test cases and their results
Manages workflow assignments between team members

content.text: Parent-child relationships are organizational tools within a tracker. They organize items in a logical hierarchy to make trackers more readable and understandable. Parent-child relationships are strictly within the same tracker — you cannot create a parent-child relationship between items in different trackers.

Parent-child relationships are organizational only and are not used for traceability. They do not cause suspected badges. Use reference relationships (upstream/downstream) for traceability between trackers.

content.prompt: Match each concept to its correct description:

pairs.right: A container that holds related items with configurable fields and workflows
An organizational hierarchy within a single tracker
A traceability link between items across different trackers
An indicator that appears when an upstream reference changes

keyTakeaways: Codebeamer organizes data into trackers — each with configurable fields, workflows, and permissions
Test management uses tracker types including Requirement, Test Case, Test Run, Bug, and Task
Parent-child relationships organize items within a single tracker but do not provide traceability
Reference relationships (upstream/downstream) drive traceability and trigger suspected badges when changes occur

topics.title: Exercise - Set Up the Test Plan

content.text: As the test lead, you will upload the test plan document to the Codebeamer project and assign a workflow task to the test engineer to begin writing test cases.
Make sure you have completed Exercise 1 (Set Up Course Project) so the PTCU Electric Car TEST project exists with the correct user roles configured.

content.title: Set Up the Test Plan
content.objective: Upload the test plan document and assign a workflow task to the test engineer.

steps.action: Open the test plan document TestPlan_AHS.docx from the course files.
steps.detail: Test plans act as a framework for outlining the methodology for testing a product. This plan covers the Advanced Headlight System.
steps.hint: The file opens in LibreOffice. Do not alter the file.

steps.action: Examine the test plan contents and identify the testing strategies.
steps.detail: The plan identifies test types, when the product is tested, and by whom. Your company may follow Agile, V-model, or a hybrid approach.

steps.action: Close LibreOffice without saving the file.
steps.detail: You have reviewed the plan and will now upload it to Codebeamer.

tasks.title: Upload the Test Plan to the Project

steps.action: Select the Documents tab of the PTCU Electric Car TEST project.
steps.detail: The Documents tab is where project files such as test plans, specifications, and guides are stored.

steps.action: Click New (+) then select File.
steps.detail: You are uploading the test plan as a project document.

steps.action: Click Choose files to upload and select the TestPlan_AHS.docx file.
steps.detail: This uploads the test lead's approved test plan to the project.

steps.action: Update the Status field to Final and click Upload.
steps.detail: Setting the status to Final indicates the test plan has been approved and is ready for use.
steps.hint: You can also use a document tracker type to manage test plans with workflow status updates and traceability.

tasks.title: Add and Assign a New Task

steps.action: Navigate to the Trackers tab and double-click the Tasks tracker from Work Items.
steps.detail: The test lead assigns workflow tasks to team members to coordinate the testing effort.

steps.action: Click Create a new item (+) and set the Summary to Write AHS Test Cases.
steps.detail: This task tells the test engineer to follow the test plan while writing test cases.

steps.action: Set the Description to Workflow task for Advanced Headlight System test cases.
steps.detail: A clear description helps the assignee understand what is expected.

steps.action: Set the Assigned to field to the Test Engineer role.
steps.detail: Click the pencil icon, select Test Engineer from Choose Roles, then click Set.

steps.action: Set the association type to related and associate the item with TestPlan_AHS.docx.
steps.detail: This links the task to the test plan document so the engineer can easily find it.
steps.hint: Edit the association type and associated item fields in the Association section.

steps.action: Save the task and verify the association in the Associations tab of the Details section.
steps.detail: The test plan document should appear as an association to the new task.

keyTakeaways: Test plans are uploaded to the project's Documents tab for team access
The test lead assigns workflow tasks to coordinate test case authoring
Tasks can be associated with documents to maintain links between work items and reference materials
Setting the document status to Final indicates the test plan is approved and ready for use

topics.title: Exercise - Structure Test Cases

content.text: As the test lead, you will implement the test plan by structuring the project's test case trackers. You will examine existing trackers, create new ones for software requirements, and organize them into their respective specification folders.

The Advanced Headlight System project needs test case trackers for system, hardware, and software requirements. System and Hardware Test Cases already exist — you will create the Software Test Cases tracker.

content.title: Structure Test Cases
content.objective: Examine existing test case trackers, create a new Software Test Cases tracker, and organize it into the correct folder.

tasks.title: Examine the Predefined Test Cases Structure

steps.action: Navigate to the Trackers tab and locate the System Test Cases tracker in the System Specifications folder.
steps.detail: The project already has test case trackers for system and hardware requirements.

steps.action: Right-click the System Test Cases tracker and select Configuration Diagram.
steps.detail: The configuration diagram shows which upstream references this tracker can verify.

steps.action: Observe that System Test Cases can verify upstream references of User Stories, System Requirements, Bugs, and Tasks.
steps.detail: These relationships define the traceability between test cases and the items they validate.

steps.action: Close the diagram, then view the Hardware Test Cases Configuration Diagram similarly.
steps.detail: Hardware Test Cases verify upstream references of Hardware Requirements, Bugs, and Tasks.
steps.hint: Right-click the Hardware Test Cases tracker in the Hardware Specifications folder.

steps.action: Close the Configuration Diagram.
steps.detail: You now understand how existing test case trackers are configured.

tasks.title: Create a Software Test Cases Tracker

steps.action: From the toolbar, click New Tracker (+).
steps.detail: You are creating a new tracker to hold test cases for software requirements.

steps.action: Set Type to Test Case, Name to Software Test Cases, Key to SOTESTCASE, and Description to Verify software requirements.
steps.detail: The tracker type determines its default fields and capabilities. Test Case trackers include test steps and verification fields.

steps.action: Click Save and select the Fields tab.
steps.detail: You need to configure which requirements this tracker verifies.

steps.action: Search for verifies and click the Work/Config Items option.
steps.detail: This field defines the traceability link between test cases and requirements.

steps.action: Under the project bullet, click the More dropdown and select the last Work Items option. Then set it to Software Requirements.
steps.detail: This configures the tracker so its test cases can verify Software Requirements specifically.
steps.hint: Use the second indented bullet's More dropdown, not the first.

steps.action: Click OK and save the new tracker.
steps.detail: The Software Test Cases tracker is now configured to verify Software Requirements.

steps.action: Navigate to the Trackers tab and locate the new Software Test Cases tracker at the bottom of the tracker tree.
steps.detail: New trackers are added at the bottom and need to be organized into the correct folder.

steps.action: Drag the Software Test Cases tracker to the Software Specifications folder.
steps.detail: Organizing trackers into their respective specification folders keeps the project structure clean and logical.
steps.hint: Use the arrow feedback to ensure you drop the tracker into the correct folder.

steps.action: Confirm the Software Test Cases' Configuration Diagram now verifies Software Requirements.
steps.detail: Right-click the tracker and select Configuration Diagram to verify the upstream reference is correctly configured.

keyTakeaways: Test case trackers are configured to verify specific requirement types through the Verifies field
The Configuration Diagram shows upstream and downstream traceability relationships between trackers
New trackers should be organized into their respective specification folders for a clean project structure
Each specification area (system, hardware, software) has its own test case tracker
